text: Maximal anaerobic running capacity (V_MART) correlates more strongly with 5 km running velocity than VO2max does in well-trained runners (r=0.77 vs r=0.49)
Anaerobic power above VO2max combined with VO2max explains 73% of the variation in 5 km running velocity, indicating both aerobic and anaerobic capacity are critical for 5 km performance
Running economy is strongly related to the capacity to produce power above VO2max (r=0.73), suggesting neuromuscular power training may improve economy

Higher muscle activation relative to maximal sprint capacity (AEMG ratio) during the ground contact phase at 3 km correlates with faster 5 km performance (r=0.60), indicating neural drive contributes to distance running speed
Distance running performance and running economy are related to neuromuscular capacity to produce force, supporting the inclusion of maximal sprint and power work in 5 km training programs
